25.1.label: Question 25.1
25.1.scenario: Medical conditions can be classified as chronic or acute. Medical services can be classified as primary, secondary or tertiary. In North America, insurers commonly provide cover for all conditions treated by all medical services. In the UK, private medical insurance (PMI) insurance has usually been restricted to acute conditions treated by secondary or tertiary services. You are the actuary to a leading provider of group and individual PMI in the UK. You have been investigating the difficulties that have arisen as a result of ambiguities in the classification of medical conditions between acute and chronic.
25.1.question: N/A

(i).question: Describe the differences between these classifications.

Differences between classifications: A chronic condition is a disease, illness or injury that has at least one of the following characteristics:
it needs long-term monitoring via consultations, check-ups, etc
it needs long-term or long-term control or relief of symptoms
it needs long-term control or relief of symptoms
the patient needs to be rehabilitated or the patient (or a carer) needs to be specially trained to cope with it
it can come back or is likely to come back.
An acute condition is a disease, illness or injury:
that is likely to respond quickly to treatment
where the treatment returns the patient to the state of health they were in before suffering the condition or the treatment leads to a full recovery.
Primary care is advice and treatment as provided by a general practitioner.
Secondary care is advice and treatment as provided by hospitals, consultants and other specialists, usually after referral by the patient's general practitioner.

Tertiary care is specialized care (usually on referral from secondary medical care personnel), by specialists working in a centre that has personnel and facilities for special investigation and treatment (eg treatment in a specialist neurological unit).

(ii).question:
In North America, insurers commonly provide cover for all conditions treated by all medical services. In the UK, private medical insurance (PMI) insurance has usually been restricted to acute conditions treated by secondary or tertiary services.

Explain why cover in the UK may be restricted in this way.

Restrictions on cover of UK policies: PMI policies in any market should be designed to match the risks faced by consumers in that market.
In the UK, primary health services are free at the point of use. The services are provided universally, with little delay, and are usually of high quality.
So, consumers face little or no risk in obtaining timely and effective primary health care.
There is a small market for 'fee for service' provision, eg 'walk in' consultation facilities at major railway stations.
This developments in response to demand from some customers for the convenience, eg being able to see a doctor outside working hours.
While secondary and tertiary care in the UK is largely available free from the NHS, there is restricted consumer choice as to where and when such care is provided.
Delays in obtaining treatment have been a particular issue. This has generated a need for private insurance.
The NHS is recognised as providing a high standard of treatment and care for chronic conditions, such as long-term cancer, diabetes and arthritis.
This treatment is supplemented by care from social services, eg home helps.
It could therefore be argued that the need for cover for chronic conditions is limited.
For insurers to cover chronic conditions, this would:
increase their overall claims costs significantly
make it more difficult and expensive to control claims, as they will last a long time
require greater reserves and solvency capital and make determining these more difficult
increase the variability of claims, hence increase the requirement for greater margins and/or reinsurance.
All of the above will adversely affect profits and / or lead to significantly higher premium levels that are unlikely to be attractive.

(iii).question:
You are the actuary to a leading provider of group and individual PMI in the UK. You have been investigating the difficulties that have arisen as a result of ambiguities in the classification of medical conditions between acute and chronic.

Describe the difficulties that may have arisen as a result of the ambiguities.

Difficulties that may have arisen: Many PMI policyholders have chosen to pay for protection over and above the NHS cover they have already paid for (through taxes). The majority of PMI policyholders therefore have high expectations.
An insurer is unlikely to make efforts to dampen these expectations, particularly if this is not common practice among other insurers.
The sales process is unlikely to emphasise the restrictions of the cover provided, despite the fact that these are part of the sales documentation.
The terms 'chronic' and 'acute' have a definite meaning for the insurer, but the insured is unlikely to understand the full implications of these terms.
Dictionary definitions are unlikely to help. Real life examples are more meaningful, but these cannot be incorporated into legal policy wording.
Many conditions have an acute phase. Some conditions (such as asthma) move to a chronic phase very quickly. Other (such as cancers) are likely to have a much longer acute phase.
The insurer needs to decide when the transition occurs, and this can be difficult.
In making this decision, the insurer needs to be aware that:
the decision will mean a change of treatment regime, probably from private to NHS provision
the decision is in effect telling the insured that there is no cure for their condition
the insured's medical advisors may be maintaining a positive view of treatment outcomes that is at variance with the outcome being implied by the insurer
the transition from acute to chronic is not that important to the insured's medical advisor - they are more concerned with treating the current condition.
A consequence of these ambiguities is more disputed claims, resulting in increased expenses and potentially bad publicity for the insurer.
In a disputed case, it may be more likely that the complaint by the insured will be upheld.

(iv).question:
It has been suggested to you that one way to overcome some of these difficulties is for your company to tighten its policy wording so that it is clearer that only treatment for acute conditions will be covered.

(a) Discuss the suggestion to tighten policy wording so that it is clearer that only treatment for acute conditions will be covered. (b) Describe the strategies that could be adopted by an insurance industry to overcome this issue.

(a) Tighten policy wording: This should reduce the number of claims and may lead to a reduction in the number of disputed claims.
However, this might be seen by current policyholders as a reduction in cover.
While changing policy wording is legal because PMI is annually renewable, it is likely to encourage current policyholders to seek cover elsewhere.
The change may also result in bad publicity for the insurer.
This may have the effect of reducing business volumes with a consequent reduction in the expected profitability of the portfolio.
There will also be a selective effect because only those policyholders in good health and without pre-existing conditions will be able to obtain comparable cover from other insurers.
This action alone fails to address the insurer's duty to treat customers fairly.
Policyholders will still need to be made aware of exactly what cover is being offered and that they have expectations that reflect the terms and conditions of the policy.
Some policyholders are still likely to be dissatisfied and the Ombudsman may still decide in favour of the policyholder in disputed cases.
There will be costs involved in deciding on and implementing this change.
(b) Strategies to overcome the issue: The industry could adopt recommended standard policy wording for the terms acute and chronic.
Documents distributed at the time of the initial sale and at each renewal could explain the restrictions of cover.
These could be in a prescribed format and include extensive case studies.
There could be recommended procedures for making the decision about the transition from an acute to a chronic condition.
The procedures may recognise that the decision is made over a period of time and is not an instant decision that comes as a surprise to the insured.
Throughout the period of a claim the nature of the cover available should be emphasised.
The insured should be made aware that cover is still available for future acute relapses of the current condition, and also for other acute conditions.

Individual insurers could build on these industry-wide strategies by adapting their counselling and advice services to ensure that the insured makes a smooth transition from acute care provided by private sector to chronic care provided by the State.
Representatives from PMI providers should meet regularly to share experiences and regularly review and, if necessary, update the standard terms.
They should also consult with other bodies, such as medical associations, consumer associations and charities (eg cancer charities).
